title: Uber Payments Platform - Real-World System Design
description: A real-world system-design walkthrough based on public material about Uber's next-generation payments platform. The design starts with a business-neutral payments API, then builds an immutable zero-sum order model, idempotent order state, Kafka-backed stream processing, PSP and bank gateways, reconciliation, cash and wallet market adaptations, and operational rollout controls.

nodes.label: Line-of-Business Systems
nodes.description: Rides, Eats, Freight, and other product systems that request collections, disbursements, refunds, tips, and market-specific payment flows.

nodes.label: Payments API
nodes.description: Uniform entry point for payment orders, profile operations, callbacks, and business-line configuration.

nodes.label: Payment Configuration
nodes.description: Self-service configuration for business lines, geographies, payment methods, PSP routing rules, and rollout controls.

nodes.label: Payment Profile Service
nodes.description: Stores and resolves tokenized payment instruments, wallet identifiers, and regional payment method metadata.

nodes.label: Risk Engine
nodes.description: Scores payment attempts for fraud, authorization risk, collection risk, and market-specific controls.

nodes.label: Payment Auth Service
nodes.description: Runs authorization, void, capture, refund, and fallback decision logic around a payment order.

nodes.label: Payment Order Service
nodes.description: Normalizes line-of-business requests into immutable zero-sum payment orders and controls the order state machine.

nodes.label: Order Store
nodes.description: Authoritative record of payment orders, state transitions, idempotency decisions, and immutable order payloads.

nodes.label: Account / Entry Store
nodes.description: Double-entry account and entry ledger for customers, partners, Uber businesses, PSP clearing, cash, and wallet accounts.

nodes.label: Payment Event Stream
nodes.description: Durable Kafka-backed stream carrying order state changes, processor commands, PSP responses, reconciliation signals, and analytics events.

nodes.label: Payment Processors
nodes.description: Horizontally scaled consumers that execute order steps, call domain services, emit side effects, and advance payment states.

nodes.label: External Request Store
nodes.description: Stores external operation IDs, PSP binding, request metadata, retry state, and provider response snapshots for idempotent integrations.

nodes.label: PSP Router
nodes.description: Chooses the provider, bank, or batch rail according to configuration, market rules, previous request binding, and fallback policy.

nodes.label: PSP and Bank Gateways
nodes.description: Adapter layer for REST PSPs, bank file exchange, callbacks, settlement files, and provider-specific behavior.

nodes.label: PSPs and Banks
nodes.description: External payment processors, wallets, banks, card networks, and legacy batch rails.

nodes.label: Cash and Wallet Service
nodes.description: Market-specific cash, wallet, arrears, top-up, and offline settlement flows such as restaurant cash drops.

nodes.label: Side-Effect Store
nodes.description: Expected and observed external effects, callbacks, ledger postings, settlement lines, and validation evidence.

nodes.label: Validator and Reconciliation Workers
nodes.description: Checks zero-sum orders, compares expected side effects with actual provider outcomes, finds dangling authorizations, and raises repair cases.

nodes.label: Reporting, Invoicing and Finance
nodes.description: Downstream financial reporting, invoices, statements, partner payout exports, tax, and accounting consumers.

nodes.label: Payment Observability
nodes.description: Dashboards, alerts, anomaly detection, logs, metrics, regional rollout views, DLQ inspection, and audit reporting.

capacityDiagram: Orders[Dozens of millions of payment transactions per day]
PSP[Many PSPs, banks, wallets, and cash flows]

functional: Expose one reusable payments platform to multiple Uber businesses such as Rides, Eats, Freight, and future lines of business.
Support common flows: onboarding payment options, authorization, capture, void, refund, tipping, dunning, payment-method fallback, and disbursement.
Represent marketplace money movement across customers, partners, Uber entities, PSP clearing, wallets, and cash accounts.
Integrate with multiple PSPs and banks through both near-real-time APIs and lower-frequency batch files.
Support market-specific mechanisms such as cash payments, digital wallets, arrears collection, and offline client behavior.
Give finance and operations teams audit, reporting, reconciliation, and repair workflows.
nonFunctional: Preserve financial correctness: no double charges, lost captures, dangling authorizations, or invented money.
Make every money movement auditable through immutable order and ledger history.
Scale to global traffic, uneven demand, many regions, many payment methods, and provider-specific behavior.
Process long-running and failure-prone work asynchronously without losing or duplicating side effects.
Use idempotency and strong application-level state transitions instead of trusting transport delivery alone.
Degrade and recover safely from provider timeouts, poison messages, partial failures, bad deployments, and external settlement delays.
Support gradual, observable rollout of new payment methods, providers, and markets.

capacity.label: Business scope
capacity.value: many Uber lines of business and geographies
capacity.note: The platform should be reusable across Rides, Eats, Freight, and future products rather than rebuilt per product.

capacity.label: Transaction volume
capacity.value: dozens of millions of payment transactions per day
capacity.note: The Under the Hood case study cites this order of magnitude, making streaming and horizontal processor scaling central.

capacity.label: External rails
capacity.value: many PSPs, banks, wallets, and cash flows
capacity.note: The platform needs both real-time API integrations and deterministic batch/file integrations.

capacity.label: Correctness budget
capacity.note: A payment can be asynchronous within a product time bound; it cannot be double-executed or silently lost.

capacity.label: Operational segmentation
capacity.value: by provider, operation, business line, country, city, payment method
capacity.note: Payment failures are often local to a provider, market, or method, so aggregate health is not enough.

api.description: Create a business-neutral payment order for collection, disbursement, refund, tip, cash settlement, or wallet movement.
Receive PSP callbacks, bank settlement acknowledgements, or wallet provider events.
Read order state, entries, provider requests, and validation status for support, operations, and finance workflows.
Record market-specific cash, wallet, arrears, or offline settlement events while preserving the shared ledger model.

dataModel.name: PaymentOrder
dataModel.notes: Immutable logical order plus guarded state transitions. Corrections are additional orders, not destructive edits.

dataModel.name: Account
dataModel.notes: Represents customers, partners, Uber entities, PSP clearing accounts, cash arrears, and wallets.

dataModel.name: Entry
dataModel.notes: A debit or credit. Entries in one order must sum to zero per currency and accounting scope.

dataModel.name: ExternalRequest
dataModel.notes: Binds retries and ambiguous failures to the original PSP or bank operation.

dataModel.name: SideEffect
dataModel.notes: Evidence used by validators to compare expected effects with provider callbacks, settlements, and ledger postings.

dataModel.name: PaymentConfiguration
dataModel.notes: Self-service config that keeps the core platform reusable across businesses, providers, and geographies.

patterns.name: Platform hourglass
patterns.what: A narrow reusable payments platform bridges many business lines to many external rails.
patterns.whenToUse: Use when products need shared capabilities but local market and provider behavior vary.
patterns.group: Start with a Business-Neutral Payments API

patterns.name: Configuration over specialization
patterns.what: Use data-driven product, geography, method, provider, and rollout rules instead of forking code per market.
patterns.whenToUse: Use when the same platform capability must behave differently across countries and business lines.
patterns.group: Start with a Business-Neutral Payments API

patterns.name: Double-entry ledger
patterns.what: Represent every money movement as balanced account entries in an immutable order.
patterns.whenToUse: Use when correctness, auditability, and reconciliation dominate availability.
patterns.group: Model Money as Immutable Zero-Sum Orders

patterns.name: Immutable event history
patterns.what: Append corrective facts instead of rewriting financial history.
patterns.whenToUse: Use when auditability matters more than simplifying the latest state.
patterns.group: Model Money as Immutable Zero-Sum Orders

patterns.name: Idempotent write API
patterns.what: Persist request identity and result so retries converge on one logical effect.
patterns.whenToUse: Use when clients or workers retry operations that can move money.
patterns.group: Enforce Idempotent Order State and Optimistic Concurrency

patterns.name: Optimistic concurrency
patterns.what: Guard order transitions with expected state and version checks instead of distributed locks.
patterns.whenToUse: Use when many processors may observe the same work but only one transition should win.
patterns.group: Enforce Idempotent Order State and Optimistic Concurrency

patterns.name: Durable event stream
patterns.what: Use Kafka-style logs to carry asynchronous work and state changes through a payment pipeline.
patterns.whenToUse: Use when processing is high-volume, retry-heavy, and too slow or fragile for one synchronous call.
patterns.group: Move Long-Running Payment Work onto Kafka Streams

patterns.name: Competing consumers
patterns.what: Scale processors horizontally by letting workers claim work from stream partitions or consumer groups.
patterns.whenToUse: Use when throughput needs to grow without adding product-facing latency.
patterns.group: Move Long-Running Payment Work onto Kafka Streams

patterns.name: Idempotent consumer
patterns.what: Make repeated event handling converge on the same order state and side effects.
patterns.whenToUse: Use with at-least-once streams, retries, replays, and poison-message recovery.
patterns.group: Move Long-Running Payment Work onto Kafka Streams

patterns.name: External idempotency
patterns.what: Bind retries to the same provider and external operation ID after ambiguous failures.
patterns.whenToUse: Use when one operation can be sent to several PSPs or banks but must not execute twice.
patterns.group: Integrate PSPs and Banks Through Idempotent Gateways

patterns.name: Provider adapter
patterns.what: Hide provider-specific APIs, callbacks, files, status codes, and retry semantics behind normalized gateway behavior.
patterns.whenToUse: Use when the platform integrates with many PSPs, banks, wallets, and local rails.
patterns.group: Integrate PSPs and Banks Through Idempotent Gateways

patterns.name: Request metadata store
patterns.what: Persist provider binding, external operation IDs, request hashes, retry state, and response evidence.
patterns.whenToUse: Use when network failures leave the external operation outcome unknown.
patterns.group: Integrate PSPs and Banks Through Idempotent Gateways

patterns.name: Side-effect validation and reconciliation
patterns.what: Record expected effects and compare them with provider, ledger, and settlement outcomes.
patterns.whenToUse: Use when external systems, async work, and money movement create latent correctness risk.
patterns.group: Validate Side Effects and Reconcile Continuously

patterns.name: Side-effect ledger
patterns.what: Record intended provider calls, ledger postings, files, callbacks, and other externally observable effects.
patterns.whenToUse: Use when logs are insufficient evidence for financial correctness.
patterns.group: Validate Side Effects and Reconcile Continuously

patterns.name: Reconciliation loop
patterns.what: Continuously compare expected payment effects against observed provider, ledger, settlement, and order state.
patterns.whenToUse: Use when external integrations can succeed, fail, timeout, or settle late.
patterns.group: Validate Side Effects and Reconcile Continuously

patterns.name: Dead-letter and repair queue
patterns.what: Move unprocessable or mismatched payment work into controlled repair paths with evidence and ownership.
patterns.whenToUse: Use when automatic retries would hide or worsen financial mismatches.
patterns.group: Validate Side Effects and Reconcile Continuously

patterns.name: Market-specific adapter
patterns.what: Contain local payment behavior in adapters while preserving the shared payment-order model.
patterns.whenToUse: Use when cash, wallets, offline flows, or local regulations differ by market.
patterns.group: Extend the Core to Cash and Digital Wallet Markets

patterns.name: Offline-first workflow
patterns.what: Let local payment actions proceed with cached or delayed state, then reconcile once connectivity returns.
patterns.whenToUse: Use for cash collection, delivery workflows, and markets with intermittent connectivity.
patterns.group: Extend the Core to Cash and Digital Wallet Markets

patterns.name: Operational dashboards
patterns.what: Expose payment health by provider, operation, country, city, method, and business line.
patterns.whenToUse: Use when aggregate success rate can mask localized payment failure.
patterns.group: Add Finance Consumers, Rollouts, and Operational Visibility

patterns.name: Gradual rollout
patterns.what: Launch payment routes and methods through configuration with health thresholds and rollback criteria.
patterns.whenToUse: Use when a bad rollout can block collections or payouts.
patterns.group: Add Finance Consumers, Rollouts, and Operational Visibility

patterns.name: Finance read models
patterns.what: Project immutable ledger and order events into reporting, invoice, tax, statement, and settlement views.
patterns.whenToUse: Use when finance needs stable operational reads without owning the write path.
patterns.group: Add Finance Consumers, Rollouts, and Operational Visibility

steps.title: 1. Start with a Business-Neutral Payments API
steps.description: Do not let every line of business integrate directly with every provider. Start with a uniform Payments API that accepts product-specific intent, resolves market configuration, payment profile, and risk context, and emits one normalized payment request. This creates the hourglass shape described in the Uber material: many businesses above, many rails below, one reusable payment platform in the middle.
steps.decisionPrompt: What must be common in the platform API, and what should stay configurable per business line, geography, and payment method?

concepts.definition: A narrow reusable payment core sits between many Uber product surfaces and many payment rails.
concepts.whyItMatters: It lets new businesses and markets reuse payment capabilities without rebuilding every PSP integration.
concepts.example: Rides, Eats, and Freight all submit orders through the same platform while using different local payment methods.
concepts.group: Start with a Business-Neutral Payments API

concepts.definition: Business lines and regions select payment methods, PSP routes, and rollout rules through configuration instead of bespoke code.
concepts.whyItMatters: Global payments need fast market expansion without making the core order model unstable.
concepts.example: Enable a wallet in India or a regional PSP in Europe without forking the charge flow.
concepts.group: Start with a Business-Neutral Payments API

whyNow: A global marketplace cannot scale if each product team owns separate payment code and PSP integrations.
The first design move is to carve out the common contract before choosing Kafka, ledgers, or external rail details.

recap.before: Business lines call payment providers directly or through product-specific adapters.
recap.after: A shared API and configuration layer normalize product requests into platform-owned payment work.
recap.newRisk: The shared API can become too generic unless the domain model underneath is precise.

flows.name: Create normalized payment request
flows.note: A product system submits intent; the platform resolves profile, configuration, and risk context before accepting work.

steps.title: 2. Model Money as Immutable Zero-Sum Orders
steps.description: Convert a payment request into an immutable order containing entries against accounts. Every order must balance to zero across customer, partner, Uber business, PSP clearing, wallet, or cash accounts. If the order is wrong, create a corrective order instead of mutating history. This is the conceptual backbone of the Uber design because it gives correctness checks even when processing is distributed and asynchronous.

options.name: Immutable zero-sum orders (double-entry) (default)
pros: Every order must balance to zero across customer, partner, Uber, PSP, wallet, and cash accounts, so missing or duplicated money movement is detectable.
Immutable history means corrections are new corrective orders, keeping an explainable audit trail.
Gives the later reconciliation worker a concrete invariant to check even when processing is distributed and asynchronous.
cons: More records to write per payment than a single balance update.
Reading a current balance means deriving it from entries (or a materialized view).
Modeling every flow as balanced entries is more upfront design work.

options.name: Mutable per-account balance updates
pros: Simple and fast: a payment just increments/decrements account balances in place.
Current balance is a direct read with no derivation.
Fewer rows written per transaction.
cons: No zero-sum invariant to catch a missing or duplicated movement across distributed processors.
Editing balances in place destroys history, so audits and disputes are hard to explain.
Corrections mutate state rather than appending a corrective order, increasing the chance of silent error.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why should a marketplace payment be represented as a balanced order instead of a mutable balance update?

concepts.definition: A durable intent plus a set of money movements that are not edited after creation.
concepts.whyItMatters: Immutability keeps audit trails explainable and makes repair flows explicit.
concepts.example: A tip correction creates another order rather than editing the original trip charge.
concepts.group: Model Money as Immutable Zero-Sum Orders

concepts.definition: The sum of debits and credits inside an order must be zero.
concepts.whyItMatters: It detects missing or duplicated money movement even if the order is processed by several services.
concepts.example: Customer debit equals driver credit plus Uber fee plus tax or clearing entries.
concepts.group: Model Money as Immutable Zero-Sum Orders

whyNow: Before adding asynchronous workers, retries, and external PSP calls, the platform needs an invariant that tells it whether money moved correctly.
The zero-sum model gives the later reconciliation worker a concrete rule to check.

recap.before: The platform has a common API but no durable money model.
recap.after: Every payment is represented by immutable orders, accounts, and entries.
recap.newRisk: The model must handle local payment methods and cash without embedding one market's assumptions into the core.

flows.name: Post a balanced order
flows.note: The order service persists the immutable order and posts balanced account entries before emitting downstream work.

steps.title: 3. Enforce Idempotent Order State and Optimistic Concurrency
steps.description: Payment callers and stream consumers retry. The order service therefore needs idempotency keys, stable order IDs, state transition guards, and optimistic concurrency so a replay cannot double-create or double-advance money movement. This is the first place where exact-once payment effects are designed as an application invariant, not assumed from the transport.

options.name: Idempotency key + optimistic concurrency (default)
pros: A persisted idempotency key maps retries to the original result, so a replay never double-creates money movement.
Optimistic state transitions advance an order only if its version/state match — no distributed locks held.
Scales well across many competing stream consumers with low coordination cost.
cons: Concurrent processors on the same hot order retry on version conflicts.
Idempotency scope must include semantic request equality, not just a string key.
Requires careful retry-on-conflict logic in every consumer.

options.name: Pessimistic distributed lock per order
pros: A lock on the order id serializes all processors — no version conflicts to retry.
Simple mental model: hold the lock, advance the state, release.
Predictable behavior when many consumers contend on the same order.
cons: Introduces a lock manager that must itself be highly available — a new dependency and failure mode.
A crashed holder needs lock leases/timeouts, or work stalls.
Lock acquisition latency and contention cap throughput across the processor fleet.

steps.decisionPrompt: Where do idempotency keys live, and what should be returned when the same request arrives again?

concepts.definition: A caller-provided or platform-generated key that maps retries to the original operation result.
concepts.whyItMatters: Payments prefer duplicate suppression over ambiguous retries because duplicate effects can charge or pay twice.
concepts.example: The same capture request returns the original capture result instead of creating a second capture.
concepts.group: Enforce Idempotent Order State and Optimistic Concurrency

concepts.definition: Advance an order only if the current version and state match the expected previous state.
concepts.whyItMatters: It avoids distributed locks while protecting the order state machine from concurrent consumers.
concepts.example: Move AUTHORIZED to CAPTURE_PENDING only if version 4 is still current.
concepts.group: Enforce Idempotent Order State and Optimistic Concurrency

whyNow: Once orders are durable, retries become safe only if duplicate requests converge on one logical order.
This step also prepares the order store for multiple asynchronous processors.

recap.before: The order model is correct, but duplicate requests may still duplicate effects.
recap.after: Idempotency and guarded state transitions make retries safe at the order boundary.
recap.newRisk: The idempotency scope must include semantic request equality, not just a string key.

traps.trap: Trusting Kafka or HTTP retries to provide exactly-once payment behavior.
traps.why: Transport can redeliver and callers can retry after ambiguous failures.
traps.instead: Persist idempotency decisions and order state transitions in the payment domain.

flows.name: Retry-safe order creation
flows.note: A repeated request consults the order store and returns the original result.

steps.title: 4. Move Long-Running Payment Work onto Kafka Streams
steps.description: Provider calls, settlement files, wallet updates, retries, and reconciliation are not a single synchronous request. Add Kafka as a durable event stream and horizontally scaled processors as competing consumers. The order store remains the source of truth; the stream carries work and state-change notifications. At-least-once delivery is acceptable only because processors are idempotent and state transitions are guarded.

options.name: Async Kafka stream + idempotent consumers (default)
pros: Slow, failure-prone provider work is decoupled from the API call, so the business line gets a fast acknowledgement.
Competing consumers scale each stage independently and absorb spikes.
Durable events enable replay, lag monitoring, and fanout to finance/reconciliation consumers.
cons: At-least-once delivery means consumers must be idempotent and guard state transitions.
Poison messages, consumer lag, and replay bugs become production concerns.
End-to-end completion is eventual, so callers must handle pending states.

options.name: Synchronous in-request provider processing
pros: Simplest model: the API call authorizes and captures inline and returns the final result.
No stream, no consumer fleet, no replay/lag machinery to operate.
Caller immediately knows the terminal outcome — no pending state to reconcile.
cons: Provider latency and outages are exposed directly to the business line's request.
A traffic spike or slow PSP cascades back-pressure into the API tier.
No durable work queue to replay or repair partially completed payments after a crash.

steps.decisionPrompt: Which payment work can be asynchronous, and which state must still be committed before acknowledging the business line?

concepts.definition: Durable events carry payment work between independently scaled services.
concepts.whyItMatters: It absorbs spikes, isolates provider latency, and lets the platform replay or repair work.
concepts.example: Order-created events fan out to capture processors, risk enrichment, reporting, and reconciliation.
concepts.group: Move Long-Running Payment Work onto Kafka Streams

concepts.definition: The stream may redeliver messages, so each consumer must dedupe or guard side effects.
concepts.whyItMatters: Payment correctness cannot depend on a single delivery attempt.
concepts.example: A capture processor reprocessing the same event sees the order already captured and becomes a no-op.
concepts.group: Move Long-Running Payment Work onto Kafka Streams

whyNow: After the order state is safe, the platform can decouple slow and failure-prone work from the API call.
Kafka provides the backbone for parallel processing, replay, lag monitoring, and fanout to finance consumers.

recap.before: The platform can create correct orders but still risks synchronous provider latency and overload.
recap.after: A stream-backed processor fleet advances orders asynchronously and scales each stage independently.
recap.newRisk: Poison messages, lag, replay bugs, and duplicate delivery now become production concerns.

flows.name: Asynchronous order processing
flows.note: The API commits the order, then workers consume the stream and advance the payment state.

steps.title: 5. Integrate PSPs and Banks Through Idempotent Gateways
steps.description: External payment rails are heterogeneous: modern REST PSPs respond near real time, while some banks still exchange files in batches. Add a PSP router, provider adapters, and an external request store. The request store binds an operation to one provider and one external ID so retries after ambiguous network failures go back to the same provider instead of double-charging through a fallback rail.

options.name: Provider-bound idempotent gateway + request store (default)
pros: An external request store binds an operation to one provider and one external ID, so an ambiguous-timeout retry goes back to the same PSP — never double-charges via a fallback rail.
Adapters normalize both real-time REST PSPs and batch bank files behind one order model.
Treating a network error as 'unknown' (then reconciling) avoids creating duplicate external effects.
cons: The request store is extra durable state written before every provider call.
Provider-specific quirks must be carefully normalized by adapters.
More moving parts: router, adapters, and request metadata to operate.

options.name: Fire-and-reconcile (no provider binding)
pros: Simpler call path: send the charge, then let continuous reconciliation catch any discrepancy.
No request store to persist provider/external-ID bindings before each call.
Leans on the reconciliation worker that already exists for correctness.
cons: A timeout retry can hit a fallback provider and double-charge before reconciliation runs.
Reconciliation is after-the-fact, so duplicate external money movement can briefly exist.
Disputes and refunds for duplicate charges are costly and erode trust.

steps.decisionPrompt: How do you retry safely after a network timeout when the provider may already have charged the customer?

concepts.definition: Once a payment operation chooses a PSP, ambiguous retries reuse the original provider and external operation ID.
concepts.whyItMatters: Trying a second provider after a timeout can create duplicate external effects.
concepts.example: A timeout on PSP A is retried on PSP A with the same provider idempotency key, not on PSP B.
concepts.group: Integrate PSPs and Banks Through Idempotent Gateways

concepts.definition: Provider adapters handle both transaction-at-a-time APIs and low-frequency bank files.
concepts.whyItMatters: A global platform must support modern PSPs, local wallets, and legacy settlement rails without changing the core order model.
concepts.example: Credit-card capture uses REST; a bank settlement uses deterministic file batches.
concepts.group: Integrate PSPs and Banks Through Idempotent Gateways

whyNow: The internal stream can retry safely only if external requests also have stable identities and provider-specific semantics.
This is the step that turns a payment order into real money movement outside Uber.

recap.before: Internal order processing is asynchronous and retry-safe.
recap.after: Provider calls, callbacks, and batch files are mediated by idempotent gateways and durable request metadata.
recap.newRisk: Provider-specific quirks can leak into platform semantics unless adapters normalize them carefully.

failureDrills.scenario: PSP capture request times out after the provider accepted it.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The retry reuses the same PSP and external operation ID, then reconciles provider state before any fallback.
failureDrills.mitigation: Persist provider binding before the call and treat network errors as unknown, not failed.

flows.name: Idempotent PSP capture
flows.note: The PSP router consults durable request metadata before calling an external provider.

messages.label: call PSP with external operation id

steps.title: 6. Validate Side Effects and Reconcile Continuously
steps.description: In a distributed payment platform, a functional bug can produce technically successful but financially wrong results. Add a side-effect store and reconciliation workers that compare expected effects, provider callbacks, settlement files, ledger postings, and order states. This catches duplicate payments, missing captures, dangling authorizations, currency errors, and zero-sum violations after the fact.
steps.decisionPrompt: What evidence should be recorded so the system can prove that every expected external effect actually happened once?

concepts.definition: A durable statement that a processor intended to call a provider, post entries, emit a settlement line, or update state.
concepts.whyItMatters: Validation can compare intended effects with observed effects instead of relying on logs alone.
concepts.example: Expected provider capture for order O is matched against PSP callback, settlement file, and ledger posting.
concepts.group: Validate Side Effects and Reconcile Continuously

concepts.definition: A background process that detects financial mismatches and emits repair or investigation work.
concepts.whyItMatters: No online path can eliminate every external, timing, or integration failure at global scale.
concepts.example: Find authorizations older than the allowed window that were never captured or voided.
concepts.group: Validate Side Effects and Reconcile Continuously

whyNow: External effects and asynchronous processing create uncertainty that online idempotency alone cannot fully resolve.
Reconciliation turns payment correctness into a continuously checked invariant.

recap.before: Provider integration can execute real money movement, but errors may be latent.
recap.after: Validation and reconciliation compare expected and actual effects across orders, providers, and accounts.
recap.newRisk: Repair logic must be conservative; automatic compensation can make a bad mismatch worse.

flows.name: Validate provider effect
flows.note: A validator correlates expected side effects with order state, ledger entries, and external responses.

steps.title: 7. Extend the Core to Cash and Digital Wallet Markets
steps.description: A real Uber payment platform cannot assume card rails. Some markets depend on cash, digital wallets, intermittent connectivity, restaurant cash drops, arrears collection, or offline client flows. Add a Cash and Wallet Service that maps those market-specific flows back into the same order, account, entry, and reconciliation model instead of bypassing the platform.
steps.decisionPrompt: How do cash and wallet flows stay compatible with the platform's ledger invariant while still respecting local market behavior?

concepts.definition: A service that handles local cash or wallet details while preserving the shared order and ledger contract.
concepts.whyItMatters: Global platforms need local behavior without fragmenting financial correctness.
concepts.example: Restaurant cash-drop events post entries against delivery-partner arrears and restaurant settlement accounts.
concepts.group: Extend the Core to Cash and Digital Wallet Markets

concepts.definition: Client or partner actions can proceed with cached state and synchronize later with reconciliation safeguards.
concepts.whyItMatters: Payment flows in emerging markets may occur where network connectivity is unreliable.
concepts.example: A delivery partner completes a cash drop at pickup and the app syncs the state when connectivity returns.
concepts.group: Extend the Core to Cash and Digital Wallet Markets

whyNow: After the core is correct, the platform can safely add local payment mechanisms without rewriting the central model.
Cash and wallet flows prove whether the order and account abstraction is actually business-independent.

recap.before: The platform supports electronic rails and provider callbacks.
recap.after: Cash, wallet, arrears, and offline flows map into the same ledger and reconciliation system.
recap.newRisk: Offline or physical cash events can arrive late, conflict with app state, or require manual operations.

flows.name: Cash settlement event
flows.note: A local cash flow posts account entries and emits an event that reconciliation can later verify.

steps.title: 8. Add Finance Consumers, Rollouts, and Operational Visibility
steps.description: Payments must be operated by both engineers and finance teams. Add reporting and invoicing consumers, region/payment-type dashboards, anomaly detection, DLQ review, provider health views, and rollout controls. Because PSP integrations and payment methods vary by market, production rollout is data-driven: start in safe regions, measure collection rate and failure modes, then expand.
steps.decisionPrompt: Which metrics prove that a new payment method is financially correct, operationally healthy, and safe to expand?

concepts.definition: Metrics and alerts are broken down by operation, provider, payment type, geography, and business line.
concepts.whyItMatters: A global outage may look small in aggregate while a single country or PSP is failing badly.
concepts.example: Alert on capture success rate for one wallet provider in one country, not only global capture rate.
concepts.group: Add Finance Consumers, Rollouts, and Operational Visibility

concepts.definition: Payment features launch gradually with explicit health metrics and rollback criteria.
concepts.whyItMatters: A bad rollout can lose money, double-charge users, or block partner payouts.
concepts.example: Enable a new PSP route for one city, compare authorization success, provider errors, and reconciliation cases, then expand.
concepts.group: Add Finance Consumers, Rollouts, and Operational Visibility

whyNow: The core design is not complete until finance and operations can see what happened and intervene safely.
Rollout discipline is part of payment architecture because every market and provider behaves differently.

recap.before: The platform can process and validate diverse payment orders.
recap.after: Finance, operations, and engineering have reporting, observability, and rollout controls to run the platform globally.
recap.newRisk: Too many metrics can hide the few signals that should page the on-call engineer.

flows.name: Operational rollout loop
flows.note: Configuration rolls a payment method forward while observability and finance consumers watch correctness and health signals.

finalDesign.title: Final Design - Uber-Style Streamed Payments Platform
finalDesign.description: The final design is a business-neutral payment platform that converts product-specific requests into immutable zero-sum payment orders. The Payments API resolves configuration, payment profile, and risk context before the Order Service persists an idempotent order, posts balanced account entries, and emits payment events to Kafka. Processor workers consume those events to run authorization, capture, refund, wallet, or cash flows. PSP routing binds ambiguous external operations to one provider and one external operation ID, while gateways normalize REST callbacks and batch bank files. Side-effect records and reconciliation workers compare intended effects with provider outcomes, ledger postings, and settlements. Cash and wallet adapters preserve local-market behavior without bypassing the shared order model. Reporting, invoicing, dashboards, anomaly detection, DLQ review, and gradual configuration rollout make the platform operable by engineering, finance, and regional teams.

flows.name: End-to-end card capture
flows.note: A product request becomes an immutable order, asynchronous processor work, an idempotent PSP call, ledger postings, and reconciliation evidence.

flows.name: Provider timeout repair
flows.note: Ambiguous provider outcomes are retried or reconciled through the request store instead of blindly switching PSPs.

messages.label: retry same PSP and external ID

functional.how: Payments API and configuration layer isolate product-specific intent from payment rails.
Immutable orders, order state machine, and processors handle authorization, capture, refund, tip, dunning, and disbursement flows.
Double-entry accounts and entries model customers, partners, Uber businesses, PSP clearing, cash, and wallets.
PSP router, gateways, request store, callbacks, and batch file handling normalize external rails.
Cash and Wallet Service preserves local flows while posting shared account entries.
Reporting, side-effect validation, reconciliation, and observability expose financial correctness.

nonFunctional.how: Zero-sum orders, idempotency, provider binding, and reconciliation prevent silent double or missing effects.
Immutable orders, account entries, external requests, and side-effect evidence retain explainable history.
Kafka streams and competing processors scale order work independently from product APIs.
At-least-once events are paired with idempotent consumers and guarded state transitions.
Request metadata preserves PSP binding and reconciliation handles ambiguous outcomes.
Configuration, segmented metrics, and finance read models support gradual market/provider rollout.

say: State that this is a real-world Uber-inspired case based on public sources.
Clarify that the system is a reusable payment platform for many businesses and markets, not a single checkout API.
Put correctness, auditability, and idempotency ahead of low-latency synchronous completion.
Model money with immutable zero-sum orders, accounts, and entries.
Explain why corrections are new orders, not mutations.
Move long-running work to Kafka and processors.
Describe retry-safe state transitions and idempotent consumers.
Deep dive into PSP binding and ambiguous external failures.
Extend the core to cash and wallet markets.
Close with rollout, observability, finance reporting, and how to detect money loss or duplicate effects.

expectations: Identifies API, order store, ledger, workers, and PSP integrations.
Mentions idempotency and retries but may not fully separate internal and external effects.
Usually misses side-effect validation and market-specific cash/wallet flows.
Models immutable zero-sum orders and accounts clearly.
Designs Kafka-backed processing with idempotent consumers and guarded state transitions.
Handles PSP timeout ambiguity with request storage and provider binding.
Frames the platform as a business-neutral product surface with stable domain invariants.
Separates payment correctness from transport guarantees and treats reconciliation as a first-class system.
Reasons about global rollout, market-specific adapters, external rails, finance consumers, and operational repair workflows.
Challenges every fallback path for duplicate-charge and missing-money risk.

followUps: How would you support partial capture, split tender, tips, and later chargebacks without mutating old orders?
What exactly goes into the idempotency key and request hash for order creation versus PSP capture?
How do you decide when a provider timeout should be retried, queried, reconciled, or escalated to manual review?
How would you shard the order store and account store while preserving zero-sum checks?
What dashboards would you build for a new PSP rollout in one country?
How would you migrate a business line from legacy direct PSP calls to the platform without double-processing live money?
Which operations must be strongly consistent, and which read models can be eventually consistent?
How would you test cash and wallet flows that depend on offline clients and physical money movement?

links.groupDescription: The specific source this real-world interview is based on.
links.title: Uber's Payments Platform
links.why: Synthesizes Uber public talks into the platform model: payment orders, double-entry concepts, Kafka processing, PSP integration, cash payments, reliability, and rollout practices.

links.groupDescription: Official Uber Engineering sources about the payments platform and related payment flows.
links.title: Engineering Uber's Next-Gen Payments Platform
links.why: Official summary of Uber's next-generation payments design goals, core data model, stream processing, idempotent integration, and launch-speed motivation.

links.groupDescription: Official Uber Engineering sources about the payments platform and related payment flows.
links.title: Scaling Cash Payments in Uber Eats
links.why: Shows how Uber adapted payments to cash-heavy markets, offline behavior, restaurant cash drops, and state-machine support.

links.groupDescription: Official Uber Engineering sources about the payments platform and related payment flows.
links.title: Re-Architecting Cash and Digital Wallet Payments for India with Uber Engineering
links.why: Adds market-specific context for cash, wallets, local payment providers, and regional operations constraints.

links.groupDescription: Official Uber Engineering sources about the payments platform and related payment flows.
links.title: Transforming Payments and Empowering Developers: Meet the Uber Amsterdam Tech Team
links.why: Provides broader context around Uber's global payment teams, distributed money systems, SRE, cash collection, and developer platform needs.

links.groupDescription: Uber references for stream auditing, durable workflows, and exactly-once-adjacent processing.
links.title: Introducing Chaperone: How Uber Engineering Audits Apache Kafka End-to-End
links.why: Shows Uber's Kafka audit mindset: quantify loss, duplication, lag, and cross-tier pipeline health.

links.groupDescription: Uber references for stream auditing, durable workflows, and exactly-once-adjacent processing.
links.title: Real-Time Exactly-Once Ad Event Processing with Apache Flink and Kafka
links.why: Although about ads, it is a strong Uber example of money-adjacent event processing, overcounting risk, Kafka, Flink, and exactly-once semantics.

links.groupDescription: Uber references for stream auditing, durable workflows, and exactly-once-adjacent processing.
links.title: Conducting Better Business with Uber's Open Source Orchestration Tool, Cadence
links.why: Useful companion reference for durable microservice workflows, retries, timers, and long-running business processes.
